title: Daily Routines
shortSummary: Describe your day, from waking up to going to bed, using everyday Korean verbs.
culturalNote: Daily life in Korea often runs long, with work and study cultures that prize diligence. Many people start the morning with rice-based breakfast and a strong work or school ethic, and the phrase 수고하세요 (sugohaseyo, keep up the good work) is exchanged constantly to acknowledge someone's effort. Coffee culture is enormous, so a mid-morning or afternoon 커피 (keopi) break is a social ritual as much as a caffeine fix. Evenings frequently include 야근 (yageun, working late) in office life or 학원 (hagwon, private academy) study for students. Time words like 아침 (achim, morning), 점심 (jeomsim, lunch/midday), and 저녁 (jeonyeok, evening) double as meal names, so context tells you whether someone means the time of day or the meal. Describing your routine politely uses the present tense in the -요 form throughout.

keyPhrases.english: I wake up in the morning

grammar.explanation: Korean marks the object of a verb with 을 after a consonant and 를 after a vowel. 아침 (achim, breakfast) becomes 아침을, and 텔레비전 (tellebijeon, television) becomes 텔레비전을. The object comes before the verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Using 를 after a consonant; 아침 ends in a consonant, so it takes 을.

grammar.explanation: To say when something happens, attach 에 (e) to a time word. 아침 (achim, morning) becomes 아침에 (achime, in the morning), and 밤 (bam, night) becomes 밤에 (bame, at night).

examples.en: I wake up in the morning.

grammar.commonMistake: Leaving out 에; without it the sentence loses the 'at/in that time' meaning.

dialogue.setup: Two friends, Daniel and Yuna, chat about their weekday routines.

lines.speaker: 다니엘
lines.english: What time do you usually wake up?

lines.speaker: 유나
lines.english: I wake up at seven in the morning.

lines.speaker: 다니엘
lines.english: What do you do after that?

lines.speaker: 유나
lines.english: I eat breakfast and go to work.

lines.speaker: 다니엘
lines.english: What do you usually do in the evening?

lines.speaker: 유나
lines.english: I come home and watch television.

lines.speaker: 다니엘
lines.english: That's similar to me. Keep up the good work!

quiz.question: How do you say 'I eat breakfast' in Korean?
quiz.explanation: 아침을 먹어요 (achimeul meogeoyo) means 'I eat breakfast'.

quiz.question: Which sentence means 'I sleep at night'?
quiz.explanation: 밤에 자요 (bame jayo) means 'I sleep at night'.

quiz.question: 텔레비전___ 봐요. (object particle after a consonant)
quiz.explanation: 텔레비전 ends in a consonant, so the object particle is 을, giving 텔레비전을.

quiz.question: What does the time particle 에 do in 아침에?
quiz.explanation: 에 attached to a time word means 'at/in that time', so 아침에 means 'in the morning'.

quiz.question: Arrange: 가요 / 회사에 (I go to work)
quiz.explanation: Place with 에 then verb: 회사에 가요.
